speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot my coffee in the car.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Uh-oh. Are you awake?

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date, December 23rd, 1994. The survivor, Nathan Fleischer. The interviewer, Marie Kaufman. The city, Los Angeles. The country, United States of America. Language, English. That's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nathan, would you begin by telling us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Nathan Fleischer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. August 13, 1917. Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Biecz. B-I-E-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Biecz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you just begin by letting us know a little bit about what the city of Biecz was like in your childhood? Who your family was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very small town in Poland. It was only about 120 Jewish families. It was a life very quiet. We used to have an organization, the Hashomer Hatzair. We used to-- the youngsters used to get together at night. One played cards, one talking. Like this. In the wintertime-- it was Poland. In this time, already there was a lot of snow. And that little town was on a hill, so we use to go sled riding. A lot of skiing. The transportation to school was on skis. There was a lot of snow in this time. And then in 1930-- life goes on. I used to work in a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first I used to go for to a school like for tool and dye making when I was in my age already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was what? What was the age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15, 16. And then in 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the worst. Hitler came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your-- who was in your family, the members of your family, brothers, sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. I was-- I had a mother and father. I had a brother and sister. My older brother died three days [SOB] three days after the war was finished. And my sister got killed in the same time with my parents in 1940 I think this was. Because in that time, they had a system, the Germans. They take out the youngsters, the young people that was healthy. They told them, you go to work day in and day out, your parents-- we're not going to touch the parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took out me from mine place where I was born in Biecz. They send me out-- Lwow. This was in Poland, Lwow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they take me out. I used to work there in steam machines.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, the trains didn't go like with electric over here. They have steam, steam machines. I used to work at night on that steam machines with the Germans. And I was working there-- this was already 1940 or 19-- the years I don't remember exactly. I think it was 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell-- let's go back a little bit to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get a sense of who-- tell me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father, his name, his Jewish name was Leib, Leon. My mother's name was Miriam Susan. Mine sister's name was Helen. My brother's name was Szja Fleischer. He was the oldest. Mine-- and we used to have like a grocery.

speaker: interviewer
text: A grocery store?

speaker: interviewee
text: A grocery store. And they had like wine and liquor for sale. Everything was in that place. Flour-- anything you needed to live. And then this was till the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a store that your family was-- owned or was renting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We was living not like millionaires, but a very quiet, nice life, like in a small town. And then-- then we used to go to school. I finished seven class public school. My sister used to go, used to travel to a bigger town to school by train. Jaslo-- by a bigger town. She finished a little bit better school than I do, than I finished with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I was-- oh. I was going to learn that profession, tool and dye making. In Europe, you have to go for three years, working in a place like this for nothing. And at night, I went to school, to night school. How do you call this? Night school. Like night school. Like an extension school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At night.

speaker: interviewer
text: For a trade school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trade school. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trade school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go from 5:00 to 7:00 in that school. And then-- and then till-- till the war started. The war started, and it was a different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have-- were your grandparents still alive at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell me about them? Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a grandfather. He was-- because my mother's maiden name was Liszt. Liszt. And his name was Liszt, too. Israel Liszt. Well, he-- in Europe, 67 years old in this time was old. So he died when he was 67 years old. My grandmother lived to a very old age. She was over-- I think 102, something like this-- when she died. When she died. She used to-- she used to have-- my mother was her daughter. And another-- my aunt was her daughter. When she got mad by my mother, she used to run away to the other daughter. Yeah, I remember it so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I used to come home, when I used to come home sometimes from the organization-- in Europe, 10 o'clock at night it was late-- so she told me always that she couldn't sleep the whole night. So I asked her, what time do I came home? She never knows. I tell her, you see, you slept overnight. So she took a broom and she threw me out of the house. 102 years old. So and then-- and then-- and then the war started. Were you a religious family? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well in Europe, they all was religious. I used to go to Jewish school. From the Polish public school from 8 o'clock till 1, it was the public school. And then from 1:00, right away to Jewish school. They called it Haida, like all the Yeshiva. From 1 to 6 o'clock in the school. And then you came home, you eat supper, it was a life. You got friends, you go to the organization, the Hashomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of things would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of things would you do in the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: They played cards. They played ping pong. They had billiards. They-- like youngsters they talk. We talked together. There was a bibliotek-- how do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: A library?

speaker: interviewee
text: Library, yeah. There was a library, so some would read books. It was a normal life. A normal life. And just the owner of that building where we had that place, his name was Singer. Singer. His name was Singer. Yeah. And then this was till the war. And when the war started in 1939, I still was working. Because I was working-- I was working in a place like over here in Texas where they drill oil. But they had a machine shop to fix their own machines. And I used to work in that machine shop. Still by the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Still-- and then you have to register yourself. And they took us. I stopped working. I couldn't work no more in that place. So we was working on the roads. Fixing roads, building roads. That company was Firma Kirchhoff, and another one was Firma Bager. We used to work on the roads. They used to pick us up with trucks and taking away-- it was kilometers. 15, 20 kilometers out of that town of Biecz. And at night, they brought us back home. And then I was a registered as a tool and dye maker, a machinist. And they needed machinist in the Limburg and Lwow. This was far away. I don't know, 200 kilometers from that place where I used to live. And over there I used to work by that steam machines. And this was a time-- I was there, I don't know, three months or four months. I don't remember exactly. And they liquidated all the Jews in Limburg and at Lwow. They killing them. They hanged them on the trees. So-- and they-- and they-- when they was dead, they took them by trucks. It was a concentration camp by the name of Janowska in Limburg. In that place they burned them. It was the crematorium was over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once a truck got spoiled when they carried that Jews, and they pick up six healthy boys like myself-- I was healthy-- to push that truck. And while we was pushing-- at this time, the Germans didn't have-- they didn't have-- they have police but all old ones. Because the youngsters-- everything was on the Russian front already in this time. So have a-- we have a chance and I run away from there. While we was pushing the trucks, I run away. Before-- before all of this started for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before all of this started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what clues were there for you that something was wrong? That something was going-- how did you know things were going bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I saw what's going on-- how they killed the people like dogs. So I thought, this is going to be my end, too. Because from that-- from that place where I was working, an Ostbahnhof in Lwow, they laid around. Because I used to live-- while this was going on, I was living in a place, a shul. Zrodlana [POLISH]. Zrodlana [POLISH]. Zrodlana [POLISH]-- the street of that--

speaker: interviewer
text: The street?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street of that shul was Zrodlana. And there was a shul. Not only me, there was about 20 people. We was working there. We were living in that shul. Later, that shul, they liquidated. And they took us in that camp, in that liquidation camp where they burned the people. So I know the time is going to come. They're going to do the same thing to me. So I run away. And I had in this time in that place, Lwow, I had my brother's wife's sister. She was living as a Christian, not as a Jew. Well, I had her address from before the war, where she lived. Not from before, from the war. Where she lived. Not as a Jew, as a Christian. And I went to her at night and she gave me 30 zlotys, like $30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for that money, I went from Limburg-- it was-- how do you call where the airplane landed? An airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Airport. An airport. And in there was a station, the train station, too. Dziegielow. Dziegielow was that place. Dziegielow was an airport. And I-- and there was a train station, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I couldn't go in on the station to buy a ticket because I had to have a [POLISH], a document of who I am, what I am. I didn't have this, so I gave a Polish conductor 20 zlotys, like $20, and he bought me a ticket to Sambor. Sambor was still a Jewish ghetto in this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the end of 1941, the beginning of 1942, something like this. And I went over there. And from there, the Jews, they gave me a few zlotys, money. And I took a train to the place where I was born. To the place where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Biecz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the train was staying there for a half an hour. I don't remember. So I went outside, and on the steps I was sitting. And a Ukrainian woman was going around with pickles. So I bought a pickles by her, and I ate that pickle. And in the back, they tapped me with the finger. And I look around and I see a Ukrainian policemen with an SS man, German SS. And the Ukrainian policeman tells me, I should show him the identification card. So I told him, I don't have an identification card. I had a uniform like in a gas station. I told him, I don't have the identification card. I live in that town and I had a telegram that my mother died over here, and I came to the funeral. And I left the identification card in the my Sunday suit, my holiday suit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he tapped me on the back. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: You're lucky you're not a Jew. If he would put the hand over here, he would see that I am a Jew because the heart was going like this. So he good bye and he left. And I left with that train all the way to that Polak. We used to live in the same house. And by that Polish people, I was hiding in the attic for three weeks. Very nice people. THey used to-- it was very poor. They used to-- the children used to go at night stealing prunes and apples. And to bring me on the attic to eat. And over there, I was three weeks. I couldn't stay longer because I was afraid the policeman, the German police, find out. They're going to kill him with his family they're going to kill-- and myself, too. So I went. They bought me a ticket, and I went to Krakow. In Krakow, it was a-- a labor camp. And that labor camp, the name was Prokocim. I had mine-- I know mine sister's husband was already in that camp. So while there was two walking from work, that people from that camp-- the policeman was a Jewish Ordnungsdienst. And I walked in into that-- when they was walking home, I walked in and I went with them. The policemen know that I am Jewish, and he took me into that-- there was about 50 people walking. And I walked in. And with them, I went into the camp in Prokocim. And in that camp, I was-- I don't know. This was in 1941. And then, in that camp, I got sick of typhus. And they took me. It was still in Krakow, it was a ghetto-- Ghetto C. And they took me there and into a hospital. And I was in that hospital, I don't know, two or three weeks. And they liquidated. The liquidation of Krakow came in. They liquidated that town to completely killing everything what is alive. And-- and-- while I was there, when I came-- when I walking into that camp, the same policeman what he took me into that camp, took me out of that hospital. His name was Wilek Pontel. He was a very, very healthy man. He was a Jewish policeman. By the Germans, they had Jewish policemen. They called them OD, Ordnungsdienst. So-- and he took me out of that camp. He paid money to a German policeman. There was wires, electric wires, and he took me out there. But while going out, they liquidating already that Krakow. When you see they show you this. It was kids, babies. One year, two years. They used to take out the kids from the cribs by their legs and with their heads in the wall killing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- but I came into that camp with him. It was a labor camp. And I was working there as a blacksmith because the people was working with such-- how you call it? When they dig the ground in this was. You have to make it hot and repave it. So I used to work on this. [SPEAKING POLISH] they call it in Polish. [SPEAKING POLISH]. And I was there to liquidate-- till they liquidate that camp. When they liquidate that camp, they took us to another camp. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already in 1942. This was already 1942. 1942. Yeah. And then they took us to another camp. The whole how you call it-- Fuhrer, the whole manager of that camp was Miller, an SS man by the name of Miller. And over there they took me to work to the same place I was working. Going out of the camp and working by a cement machine. Cement machine operating. And then I used to sleep on that where we was working, too. Because they had-- while I was working during the day, at night, the water used to come in there. So they used to have a water pump what was running the whole night. And I used to take care of that water pump the whole night. So I used to sleep there. I used to come only in the camp to get some food and clothes, too. And then in 1942, they liquidated that camp, too. And they took us into the regular concentration camp-- Krakau-Plaszow. They used to call it Jerozolimska. And over there, it was that professional killer, Goth. Goth. And over there, I used to work also as a blacksmith. And then I used to take care-- they have mountains. What they exploding those mountains and they have a compressor. I used to work by that compressor. And then after working there-- it was-- it was terrible because for nothing they kill you there. For instance, he went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, you walk and he calls you-- come on, come over here. So I came to him. And he tells me, look in my eyes. So you couldn't look such a bastard in the eyes, so you look away. So he tells you, [SPEAKING GERMAN] You don't have a clear conscience. Boom. He kill you for this. And then a girl was carrying stones in a carriage. Such a carriage what they carry--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a wheelbarrow?

speaker: interviewee
text: A wheelbarrow. Yeah. So she saw him walking, so she put on full. She was afraid. And that stones, when she was carrying that, the stones used to fall down. So he says, you're making sabotage. And he kill her for this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then it was-- then was times-- for instance, I was very short of being killed. I was working at that compressor. And this time, there was radiators. And they didn't have antifreeze like we have over here. It was regular water. And at night, when you went home to the barracks where you sleep, I used to let out the water. It shouldn't freeze at night. And one night, I forgot to let out the water, and I came back in the morning and the whole radiator was-- boom. So I was lucky was another compressor where the engine wasn't good, but the radiator-- the same compressor-- the radiator was good. So I changed the other radiator, that good radiator to this one. It was luck. And everything was all right. So-- and then I was working there, I don't know, three months or four months. And then was on the sign, on the Jewish police-- there was a Jewish police there. There was a sign they needed machinists, toolmakers to Mielec.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I registered myself because I have in that camp my brother. I figured out this is going to be a good thing. I'm going to be in with my brother. So they gave us already bread. When they move from one camp, they want you to be all right from one camp to another. So the next day morning, instead of going to Mielec, I see it comes a truck. On the truck was the name, Krakau-Emalia.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is where Schindler was. So I came. It took maybe 10 minutes, not even this, with the truck. It was very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came in, I see a camp. This was Schindler's camp. I see in the middle of the camp, stays a table with bread with jelly. People going in and taking. I said, what the hell is this? All of a sudden, I am liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because on the other camp from where I came, the hunger was terrible. The worst thing in the world is hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Tell me what that-- what did you had to do to deal with that hunger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you just go around, and in going, and look around. I used to lick the walls, eat the paint for hunger. Not in that camp, not what in-- I come to this in another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by Schindler-- we came to Schindler. So it was a kitchen. For most it was maybe 20 people what they moved us from that death camp to Schindler from the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the kitchen. In the kitchen was an old woman, an older woman. And she says in Jewish, children, don't run. Whenever you're hungry, come. [CRYING] Come and you're going to get to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by Schindler it was like a hotel. And I was working, they call it in Polish, tancownia. And presses. Big presses where they make hand grenades against tanks. We was working on this. So and I was working there, going to work, everything was all right. And then Schindler-- it used to be like a parent, like a father to everybody. And the barracks where we used to sleep, it was very hot that summer, so we used to sleep outside. So and over there it was reflectors with the police. The light in the eyes. They was watching nobody should runaway. So that the reflectors was in our eyes. So Schindler used to tell the police, told them, turn the reflector out the other way because my people can't sleep at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we couldn't sleep at night because Schindler was-- you don't meet a human being like him. He was very good. He was a human being. We worked for him, we gave him the work, but he treat us like human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and I was there till-- went was this? I think in the beginning of 1944. 1944. It was already 1944. And they needed-- and they took out about 200 people a transport to Mauthausen. This was a death camp, too. And I was between that 200 people from Schindler. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to Mauthausen. And Mauthausen was terrible. It was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mauthausen was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria. About-- about 120 kilometers from Vienna was the Austrian concentration camp. Over there, they have a Steinbruch. The Steinbruch-- it was steps. About 200 steps you have to go down. And later on-- because you don't have nothing on you. They just give you underwear, just shorts. Nothing over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Steinbruch is what? A quarry? You know, rocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Steinbruch is a place where they explode stones from mountains in the ground. Steinbruch, they call it. And over there, we took that stones up there, but they built a road up there. But at that Steinbruch that what they-- the stones, there was about 500 or 600 steps down. And later, you have to carry up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have to know it was about six people in the line, so you have to know where to stay. When you stay on the side, walk on the side-- you didn't eat, so you were shaky. So you moved out a little, you fell out a little bit off the line. So from that reflectors up there, the police-- boom, boom, boom. They tell you, you want to run away. And you moved out of the line because you hardly could walk. So they kill you from over there because you want to run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and I was working there maybe two months or three months. It's very hard to remember the exact time. And they transferred us to a labor camp-- St. Valentin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they used to-- what-- they used to give there when you come from work, they used to give half a quart of water with potato peels. I was so tired I didn't go for that food. I laid down on the stones there. And then a German couple come to me and tells me, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Be happy. You're going out from over here, and go in the line. So I didn't know what was going down. So I go in the line. People standing without clothing. Naked. When you see there the naked people, so you're going already, oh, to the crematorium where they're going to kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that? You knew that already?
